coredata.dc:description: In this new study, we present an intriguing development in the field of theranostics: the simplistic self-assembly of red-emissive amphiphilic porphyrin-like carbon dots (P-CDs). By harnessing their exceptional photophysical properties, we have revealed a strong candidate as the ideal photosensitizer (PS) for applications, particularly in the realm of imaging. Spanning a remarkable size average between 1–4 nm, these particles exhibit both highly stable and unparalleled emission characteristics between 650 and 715 nm in water in comparison to current carbon dots (CDs) available. Lastly, these CDs were fairly non-toxic when tested against normal human cell lines as well as were found to have favorable imaging capabilities in zebrafish embryo.

head.ce:title: The art of simplicity: Water-soluble porphyrin-like carbon dots self-assemble into mesmerizing red glow

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this new study, we present an intriguing development in the field of theranostics: the simplistic self-assembly of red-emissive amphiphilic porphyrin-like carbon dots (P-CDs). By harnessing their exceptional photophysical properties, we have revealed a strong candidate as the ideal photosensitizer (PS) for applications, particularly in the realm of imaging. Spanning a remarkable size average between 1–4 nm, these particles exhibit both highly stable and unparalleled emission characteristics between 650 and 715 nm in water in comparison to current carbon dots (CDs) available. Lastly, these CDs were fairly non-toxic when tested against normal human cell lines as well as were found to have favorable imaging capabilities in zebrafish embryo.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The quest for amphiphilic red emissive CDs beyond 650 nm is crucial for biological studies, which has remained a challenging pursuit. However, Xiong and co-workers shed light on the synthesis of red emitting CDs through a meticulous bottom-up, hydrothermal one-pot approach [1]. This groundbreaking method yielded purified products exhibiting a wide emission range spanning from 440 to 625 nm. Intriguingly, this work established a fundamental concept that reducing the band gap of the CDs leads to a remarkable bathochromic shift towards the lower red region at 625 nm. Consequently, it suggests a direct correlation between red emission and the augmentation of heteroatom species on the CD's surface [2–5]. Unveiling the intricate surface chemistry of CDs is of utmost importance for precisely tailoring red emission via heteroatom doping.

Carbon-based photosensitizer (PS) utilized in fluorescence-based imaging techniques suffer from significant limitations such as low water solubility, photostability, and complex synthesis [6]. To address this, researchers have extensively investigated porphyrins, naturally occurring compounds renowned for their extraordinary photo-physical properties, as a promising alternative PS [7–9]. These compounds exhibit a wide absorption profile spanning from the ultraviolet (UV) to near-infrared (NIR) regions. Moreover, literature has confirmed that, following excitation in the red region. In comparison to traditional fluorophores, porphyrins easily follow the required pathways to generate fluorescence, phosphorescence, and intersystem crossing when transitioning from an excited state [10,11]. Therefore, the primary motivation behind this study is to explore the potential applications of porphyrin-like CDs (P-CDs).

CDs have garnered significant attention across various biomedical applications, encompassing in vitro/in vivo imaging, biosensors, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, and photothermal therapy [12–16]. The allure lies in their remarkable characteristics: freedom from metals, high quantum yields of luminescence, low cytotoxicity in animal models, and both exceptional photo and thermal stability [17–20]. Moreover, CDs possess immense tunability and display amphiphilic behaviors contingent upon the synthesis method, rendering them invaluable for biological investigations. Surprisingly, literature on P-CDs suitable for biological studies remains scarce given the lack of water solubility and favorable photoluminescence [21–23]. Porphyrins play a crucial role as imaging agents due to their unique optical and electronic properties, making them highly versatile in various imaging techniques. One of the key advantages of porphyrins is their strong absorption and fluorescence properties in the visible and NIR regions, which allows for deep tissue penetration [24–26]. This feature is particularly valuable in non-invasive imaging modalities such as fluorescence imaging and photoacoustic imaging, where porphyrins can be used to visualize specific tissues or organs with high sensitivity and resolution. Synthetic porphyrins have been recognized for their pivotal biological, photophysical, and photochemical properties, making them promising candidates for theranostic applications [27–29]. Notably, the structure of a typical porphyrin comprises four pyrrolic units and four methene bridges, forming a coplanar cyclic molecule. The ease of functionalization and modification is another advantage of porphyrins as imaging agents. Porphyrins can be chemically modified to introduce specific targeting ligands, such as antibodies or peptides, enabling the selective imaging of specific cells or tissues [30,31]. Furthermore, their conjugation with nanoparticles or polymers can enhance stability, biocompatibility, and pharmacokinetic properties, facilitating their use in various imaging applications[32]. Furthermore, the conformation of the porphyrin can be manipulated by the size of the substituent axial ligands or peripheral substituents. Consequently, this manipulation bestows upon porphyrins a wide range of biochemical functions and facilitates inner binding [33,34].

Despite the numerous advantages, there are detrimental limitations associated with porphyrins as imaging agents. One of the challenges is their tendency towards photobleaching, leading to reduced fluorescence signals over time. In current applications involving porphyrins, the use of 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) is now a common PS in the clinical area [35,36]. 5-ALA is a non-fluorescent precursor to heme that is converted to the fluorescent product, protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) in tumor cells. Unfortunately, PpIX has its limitations as both photobleaching, and variations of the concentration levels occurring within the tumor. Photofrin, used for anticancer photodynamic therapy (PDT), was found to have a long half-life which caused skin photosensitivity for patients between a span of 2–3 months [37]. Researchers are actively addressing this limitation by developing strategies to enhance the stability and photostability of porphyrins through structural modifications and encapsulation within protective matrices. Another limitation is the potential cytotoxicity of certain porphyrins, particularly at high concentrations or prolonged exposure [38]. Careful consideration must be given to optimize the concentration and exposure time to ensure their safe use in imaging applications.

The motivation to develop porphyrin-like CDs arises from the need to address these limitations, aiming to enhance stability while simultaneously reducing toxicity. The work presented herein elucidates the self-assembly of a variety of P-CDs through pi-stacking of dipyrrolomethane derivatives followed by microwave irradiation, resulting in the formation of porphyrin moieties that emit far-red light at wavelengths between 650 and 715 nm, respectively. The first trial of P-CD synthesis resulted in two fractions of CDs, organic solvent only soluble CDs (PO-CDs) and water-soluble CDs (PW-CDs). Second, the effect of the presence of fluorine derivatives and meso-substituted ligands was studied involving 3,5-difluoro-4-formylbenzoic acid (DFBA) which produces water soluble CDs labelled as F-CDs, and the use of 4-[2-(dimethylamino)ethoxy]benzaldehyde (DAEB) resulting in CDs labelled as DAEB-CDs, respectively. Lastly, in proof of concept, PO-CDs which were insoluble in water were then surface modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI) to produce water soluble CDs while maintaining red emissive properties, namely PCD-PEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and instrumentation and experimental details
ce:section.ce:para: Full details describing the materials, instrumentation, and methods are provided in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PO-CDs, PW-CDs, F-CDs, and DAEB-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of each P-CDs were facilitated through the self-assembly of dipyrromethene moieties via the well-established Adler-Longo method in the presence of trifluoroacetic acid and is hypothesized to develop stable CDs via microwave approach [39,40]. The predicted base porphyrin model for each precursor to its respective CD has been provided in Fig. S1A& 1B. The porphyrin derivatives for both F-CD and DAEB-CD were confirmed via mass spectroscopy (MS) before microwave synthesis (Fig. S2A& 2B). First, 0.300 g of the aldehyde derivative of choice is solubilized in 40 mL of DMF in a 100 mL round bottom flask and stirred for roughly 5 min under sonication to ensure a homogenous solution at room temperature (RT). Next, 1.5 mL of pyrrole is aliquoted and stirred for roughly 5 min at RT. Then 200 µL of trifluoroacetic acid is aliquoted and stirred for roughly 72 hrs at RT and protected from ambient light. Additional steps were added for the reactions involving PW-CD and PO-CDs synthesis, 0.100 g of PDA was added into the solution and stirred for 12 hrs. Finally, 0.100 g of TAC was added into the same reaction and set to stir an additional 12 hrs. For all P-CDs after the reaction time is completed, the solution was quenched with two-parts de-ionized (DI) water and prepped for purification. The workup consists of an extraction with DCM for 4 cycles, with the organic fraction solvent evaporated via rotary evaporation until there is roughly 2 mL of solution left. Afterwards, dry loading of the solution is performed to prep the product for further purification by silica flash column with the mobile phase as 3% methanol: 3% ethyl acetate: 94% DCM (v:v:v). The fractions for each reaction collected were evaporated and tested for both the presence of CDs and solubility in aqueous solution. The water-soluble product was then placed in a 100–500 Da MWCO dialysis for 72 hrs with a change of water every 8 hrs. The purified solution was then frozen at − 40ºC and lyophilized for 72 hrs. F-CD was observed to produce a free-flowing black powder, DAEB-CDs produced a dark brown gel, and PW-CD produced a free-flowing dark brown powder. Noticeably, all products were found to be highly soluble in water with the exception of PO-CD, which was a dark black, brittle powder that was soluble in methanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of PO-CDs with PEI
ce:section.ce:para: To produce PCD-PEI, 4.52 mg of PO-CDs and 254 mg of PEI were dispersed in roughly 15 mL of methanol and sonicated for 3 min to produce a homogenous dark brown solution. The solution was then set for microwave irradiation at 500 W for 180 s. Post-microwave irradiation, a brown viscous gel was produced. Then product was found to be highly soluble in DI water and was set for further purification under dialysis (MWCO: 1 kDa) for 72 hrs to ensure the release of unreacted polymer fragments. The solution was then frozen at − 40ºC and lyophilized for 72 hrs. The PCD-PEI produced was found to be a light-brown gel.

To assess the optical properties of all P-CDs, an analysis of the UV–vis spectra was performed ( Fig. 1). For F-CD, the data reveals typical absorption bands attributed to the π − π * transitions of the CC bond and the n − π * transition of the CO bond at 230 and 315 nm, respectively. Importantly the intense band found at 420 nm accounts for the electronic absorption known as the Soret band, which is a vital optical feature that is common of porphyrins [41]. This information suggests that there is the presence of porphyrin moieties within F-CD. Small shoulders at 480 and 730 nm are attributed to increased conjugation of nitrogen-containing heterocyclic structures which the CD is composed of. An analysis of DAEB-CD provides similar information such that π − π * transitions of the CC bond and the n − π * transition of the CO bond at 230 and 340 nm, respectively. We hypothesize that the bathochromic shift of the absorbance of the n − π * transition is attributed to the increase of conjugation of heteroatoms due to meso-substituted ligand of DAEB as supported by literature [42]. The DAEB-CDs were also found to contain a more intense Soret band at 420 nm, again, indicative of porphyrin-like features. Next, the spectrum of the water-soluble PW-CDs revealed π − π * transitions of the CC bond and the n − π * transition of the CO bond at 230 and 315 nm, respectively. A lower intense Soret band was found at 420 nm with additional small shoulders at both 498 and 570 nm, respectively due to the arrangement of heterocyclic structures comprising of both sulfur and nitrogen present. As the counterpart PO-CD, which is insoluble in water, the UV spectrum had more pronounced π − π * transitions of the CC bond and the n − π * transition of the CO bond at 230 and 315 nm, respectively to that of PW-CDs. Interestingly, the Soret band found at 420 nm had an increase of intensity with noticeable shoulders at 498 and 570 nm, respectively. We believe that given the insolubility of PO-CDs in water, there are more carbonized heteroatom moieties which have a significantly reduced amount for both sulfur and nitrogen to contribute to the overall conjugated system of the CD. Hence resulting in the more prominence of peaks. Post-surface modification of PO-CD to synthesize PEI-CD (which is now water soluble) resulted in an overall decrease of absorption intensity of the π − π * transitions of the CC bond and the n − π * transition of the CO bond at 230 and 315 nm, respectively. Notably, we were still able to observe a Soret band at 420 nm and effectively lost any visible bands past 450 nm. We hypothesize that PEI-CDs have a high degree of PEI-conjugated moieties on the surface which competitively absorbs UV light, thus lowering the intensity of deeper aromatic structures within the CD rendering them unobservable. In summary for all P-CDs, strong absorbance in the near-UV is indicatory of the presence of the Soret band, which represents the S2←S0 transition of the porphyrin moiety [43]. Correspondingly, peaks observable in the visible region of the spectrum can be attributed to that of Q bands displayed by P-CDs, indicating the S1←S0 transition [44]. To support this behavior by porphyrin-like molecules, Gouterman et al. proposed a theoretical explanation for the absorbance spectrum of free-base porphyrins based on the π − π * electronic transitions between the two highest occupied (HOMO) and the two lowest unoccupied (LUMO) molecular orbitals within an 18-membered macrocyclic ring of a porphyrin [45]. Further works by Corwin et al. further posited that the absorption bands may originate from specific transitions involving lone pair electrons on the nitrogen groups of the ring [46]. Leading to n − π * transitions of lone pair electrons on the N groups of the macrocyclic ring.

The PL of P-CDs in water (apart from PO-CDs in methanol) was studied to assess the potential of the material as PS across a wide range of excitations that are typical of porphyrins (380–700 nm). Furthermore, we surmise that if there are indeed porphyrin moieties present, each P-CD should exhibit excitation dependent optical behavior [47]. An analysis of the PL of F-CD at lower excitations (380–440 nm) revealed four excitation dependent peaks at 455, 505, 600, 648, and 698 nm, respectively ( Fig. 2A). As we increased the range of excitation (460–600 nm) only two strong red-emitting peaks were observable at 648 and 698 nm, respectively which are expected of many porphyrin-like materials [48]. The analysis of DAEB-CDs revealed three red emissive peaks at 620, 655, and 715 nm, respectively at lower excitation wavelength (Fig. 2B). Similarly, as the excitation wavelength was increased, the change of PL bands we observed to favor far-red emission with an intense peak at 715 nm. We believe that the PL of DAEB-CDs in comparison reaches slightly farther into the red region due to the increase of conjugation of the meso-substituted ligand DAEB as supported in the UV–vis analysis. The PL spectrum of PW-CD was observed to have a narrow range of excitation with three main peaks at lower excitation (500, 648, and 705 nm, respectively, Fig. 2C). As the excitation wavelength was increased, all three peaks were significantly decreased and a small peak at 680 nm was present. We conjecture that with the increase of sulfur and nitrogen groups in PW-CD, there are emissive states that lead to the behavior displayed [49,50]. This hypothesis is supported by the analysis of PO-CD which is very similar to PW-CD but lacks the abundance of both sulfur and nitrogen. The PL of PO-CD was found to have a much lower range of excitation with four emissive peaks at 505, 605, 648, and 709 nm, respectively (Fig. 2D). The water-soluble counterpart to PO-CD (PCD-PEI) also had a narrow range of excitation (Fig. 2E). To our surprise, while retaining red-emissive peaks at 650 and 709 nm. PCD-PEI also started to exhibit peaks at 490, 575, and 610, nm respectively. We postulate that this characteristic is due to surface defects from PEI on the surface as previously investigated from works in our group involving surface modifications of CDs with PEI and others [51,52].

FTIR-ATR has been proven to be an appropriate method to unveil the surface functionality of CDs, showcasing functional groups that predominantly include oxygen and nitrogen. Additionally, this technique possesses a distinctive capability to delve as deep as 2 µm, granting it significant potency for qualitatively analyzing both the surface and core of CDs [53]. A comparative analysis was performed to reveal a glimpse into the surface of the P-CDs ( Fig. 3). F-CDs were found to have stretching vibrations at 1120 and 1680 cm−1 due to the presence of C-F and CO bonds, respectively on the surface of the CD. Upon deeper analysis of F-CD, the intense peaks at 1270 cm−1 are due to the asymmetric C-N stretching vibrations. This characteristic is supported by the presence of N-groups withing the macrocyclic ring of the porphyrin moieties [54]. Between 3000 and 3100 cm−1 one would expect to observe the C-H stretching of the alkenes, which make up the carbonized network of F-CDs alongside the conjugated carbons that make up the porphyrin structure. Additionally, the broad peak in between 3200 and 3580 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of O−H groups present as well as secondary N-H groups. DAEB-CDs were found to have similar peaks to that of F-CD, exhibiting peaks 1680, 1270, 3000, 3200, and 3580 cm−1, respectively, which correlate to CO, C-N, C-H stretching of alkenes, O-H, and secondary N-H functional groups correspondingly. An analysis of PW-CD was observed to have increased spectral broadening from the carbonized network, which diminishes an observable peak the medium peak at 2850 cm−1 indicative of C−H stretching vibrations of alkane groups [55]. Small peaks were observed at 1020 and 1680 cm−1 to support the presence of CO and C-N groups, respectively. PO-CD also displayed the same behavior of spectral broadening, which was expected as both fractions were derived from the same reaction, hence it is expected that some features may overlap. The peaks observed were identical to that of PW-CD with peaks at 1020 and 1680 cm−1 that coincide with the presence of CO and C-N groups, respectively. In the same rationale, PCD-PEI also displayed spectral broadening and the same peaks as PO-CD, though due to PEI present during the synthetic process, very broad peaks at 1580 cm−1, suggesting the availability of these groups on the surface of the PCD-PEI CDs, respectively as compared to the spectrum of PEI which exhibits peaks in the same region. It is hypothesized the abundance of these groups is responsible for the high solubility of PCD-PEI CDs in water. To assist in the confirmation of functional groups within this FTIR assessment, XPS analysis was conducted to provide more in-depth elemental information.

To further understand the surface composition of the main P-CDs (F-CD, DAEB-CD, PW-CD, and PO-CD, respectively), the CDs were analysed by using XPS and high-resolution spectra for each relevant element are reported together with table functionalities abundance distribution as summarized in
Tables 1A and 1 B. The XPS spectra for each corresponding P-CDs is provided in the supporting information (Figs. S3–5).

F-CD was observed to have an elemental composition with a carbon amount of up to 64.9% with a considerable amount of oxygen, nitrogen and fluorine (Fig. S3A-D). As shown, C1s signal shows the presence of 71% of sp2 hybridized carbon (284.8 eV), 22% of C-X (X: O, N, F) peaked at 285.7 eV and 7% of COOH (289.9 eV). O1s signal shows also the presence of both C-O (531.1 eV) and COOH (532.4 eV) with 29% and 71% relative abundance, respectively. Curiously, the precursor did not show the presence of any C-O functions that could be formed through radical cleavage and rearrangement as will be discussed in the DAEB-CDs. N1s show a single component due to pyrrolic nitrogen (N5) atoms of porphyrin ring peaked 399.7 eV as F1s where only one peak due to aromatic C-F was detected at 688.2 eV. DAEB-CDs show an elemental composition with a very high carbon amount of up to 90.6% and a small amount of oxygen and nitrogen (Fig. S4A-C). The C1s signal shows the presence of 83% of sp2 hybridized carbon (284.7 eV), 13% of C-X (X: O, N) peaked at 285.9 eV and 4% of COOH (289.8 eV). Additionally, O1s signal shows also the presence of both C-O (531.9 eV) and COOH (533.2 eV) with 98% and 2% relative abundance. Last, N1s show the presence of N5 atoms (porphyrin rings) peaked 399.7 eV and C-N peaked at 400.4 eV with a relative abundance of 58% and 42% respectively. PW-CDs were found to exhibit an elemental composition with a low carbon amount of up to 49.9%, high amounts of oxygen (40.8%) and nitrogen (6.9%) and a low amount of sulfur (Fig. S5A-D). As revealed, C1s signal shows some traces of sp3 hybridized carbon (283.7 eV, 2%), a relevant amount of both sp2 hybridized carbon (284.6 eV, 56%) and C-X (X: O, S, N) peaked at 286.5 eV (31%) and COOH (290.1 eV) up to 12%. Interestingly, O1s show both C-O (531.7 eV) and COOH (533.3 eV) with 41% and 59% relative abundance while N1s shows only the peak due to N5 atoms (porphyrin rings) at 399.8 eV. The sp2 signal shows an interesting composition with the presence of thiol species (169.2 eV, 41%) as in the precursors used together with oxidized sulphur species such sulphonyl (170.8 eV, 34%) and sulphones (172.2 eV, 17%). To our surprise, considering the insolubility in water compared to its counterpart (PW-CD) PO-CD was considerably different with a greater carbon content of up to 69.5% and lower amount of heteroatoms (O 28.9%, N 1.1%) and traces of sulphur (< 1%) in Fig. S5E-H. Furthermore, C1s signal shows the massive presence of sp3 hybridized carbon (283.6 eV, 29%) with similar sp2 hybridized carbon (284.6 eV, 53%) compared with PW-CD and only 14% of C-X (X: O, S, N) peaked at 285.9 eV and COOH (289.9 eV) up to 4%. In following, O1s show only a well-defined peak due to COOH (533.1 eV) while both N1s and S2p show very noisy signal with the possible presence of N5 and thiols, respectively.

Keeping in mind the surface analysis of each main P-CD, we have proposed both a reasonable and informed mechanism pathway which contributes towards a model to understand the structure of P-CDs. To preface, a quite constant distribution of N5 species in the samples F-CD, DAEB-CD, PW-CD, and PO-CD is observed. Furthermore, the spectroscopy analysis showed the preservation of molecular structure of the precursors. So, we conclude that the condensation of the CDs occurred by reactions involving the side chains of porphyrins moieties. As reported in Fig. S6, F-CD could be formed by a simple radical cleavage of the carboxylic functions that led to the formation of dimeric forms or trimeric ones by reacting with carboxylic functions. These trimeric species are accountable for the double charged species observed through mass spectroscopy due to the cleavage of the two condensed fragments from the central one. Similarly, DAEB-CD can be originated through heterolytic cleavage of C-O and C-N as shown in Fig. S7. The several radicals formed can condensed together forming 2–4 units large CD with the porphyrin moieties left untouched. Furthermore, the radical formed induced the formation of CDs with quite uniform functions resembling the chemical nature of the porphyrin precursors. The formation mechanisms of both F-CD and DAEB-CD show intrinsic limitation due to the steric hindrance of the precursors. The small clusters can be further arranged in supramolecular aggregates reaching the dimension of 1–2 nm as reported by TEM analysis. The reaction pathway formation of PW-CD and PO-CD are more complex as shown in Fig. S8. The side chain of the precursor originated a complex family of radical species leading to the condensation of amide and amino rich rigid species reasonably soluble in water. Alternatively, sp3 rich specie with more flexible chains and a higher hydrophobicity can be formed by losing sulphur moieties. The sulphur rich CDs can undergo to oxidation due to the radical rich environment originated by the microwave stimulated reaction environment. The spectral analysis provided together with the XPS investigation allowed to produce a simple radical based formation mechanism for all porphyrin CDs provided. The hypothesized reactivity and steric hindrance of precursors limiting the growth of CDs allowing the production of species with a size distribution lower than 3 nm. All CDs can be classified as polymeric carbon dots formed by connected porphyrin rings.

TEM images were studied regarding all P-CDs to understand the X − Y plane size distribution. The sample was sonicated for 15 min prior to measurements to breakdown any aggregate formation. F-CDs showed a narrow Poisson size distribution of 1.0–4.0 nm with a mean size of 2 nm ( Fig. 4A). The TEM of DAEB-CD had a Poisson size distribution of 1.0–8.0 nm with a slightly bigger mean size of 4.0 nm which is attributed to the aggregation behaviors of the CD (Fig. 4B). In following, the TEM of PW-CD was found to have had a Poisson size distribution of 2.0–7.0 nm with a mean size of 3.7 nm (Fig. 4C). Whereas the TEM of PO-CD was to have a Poisson size distribution of 1.0–2.5 nm with a mean size of 1.4 nm (Fig. 4D). We hypothesize this change of size in comparison to PW-CD is due to the increased carbonization of the particle and decreased availability of heteroatoms to form robust heterocycles as confirmed by FTIR and XPS analyses. The TEM of PCD-PEI reveals a size range between 1.0–6.0 nm with an average particle size of 1.7 nm (Fig. 4E). Each histogram for all P-CDs comprises of a particle count of over 300 particles with a high degree of uniformity. AFM was also performed on each CD to understand the height profile in the z-axis. The AFM images for each P-CD (F-,DAEB-,PW-,PO-, and PCD-PEI CDs) displayed particles heights within ± 1.5 nm, which is consistent with the previous TEM average diameter distribution confirming the quasi-spherical structure of the P-CDs.

The ZP of the P-CDs was determined to gain a better understanding of their surface properties and surface charge (Fig. S9). ZP is a crucial technique for characterizing various surface aspects of CDs, including charge, stability, and functionalization. By measuring the ZP, researchers can garner important insights into the electrostatic interactions between the CDs and their surrounding environment [56]. Such information plays a vital role in comprehending how CDs behave in different applications like drug delivery, imaging, and sensing [57]. The ZP of F-CDs was found to be highly negative with a value of − 50.0 mV. The negative value can be attributed to the presence of the highly electronegative fluorine throughout the CD as confirmed by XPS. We posit that fluorine in this case, must be exposed on the surface of the CD. On the other hand, DAEB-CDs exhibited a positive ZP of + 10.3 mV. We believe that there is a high abundance of DAEB fragments which contain amine groups that coat the surface of the CD. PW-CD had a ZP of − 6.3 mV, which was interesting as XPS confirms a high degree of oxygen. We hypothesize that the negative charges are shielded by nitrogen moieties which hinder a more highly negative charge on the surface. The basis of this statement is followed by the ZP of PO-CD which was observed to have a much more negative value of − 32.8 mV as compared to its counterpart PW-CD (−6.3 mV). Knowing that the XPS results confirms a much lower nitrogen content, we postulate that many oxygen moieties can be found on the surface of the CD which contributes towards the behavior observed. Following suite to further reinforced the argument of nitrogen contributing towards either a less negative or positively charged CD, PCD-PEI was found to have a ZP of + 9.6 mV. This data agrees with the hypothesis which necessitates that an increased amount of surface amines present in CDs contributes to the positive charge of the material.

In proof of concept for biological applications, the cytotoxicity of the three main P-CDs were determined using two human non-tumor cells lines, MSCs and VSMCs, respectively. The cells were exposed to increasing concentrations and viability was determined 72 hrs later. Overall, the P-CDs were observed to be fairly non-toxic, inducing little or no cell death (Fig. S10A&B). The cell viability of DAEB-CD was found to be the highest at almost all concentrations. With a treatment of 10 µg/mL, the cell viability was approximately 96% and 110% for MSC and VSMC, respectively. As the concentration increased to 100 µg/mL, the DAEB-CD’s cell viability increased to roughly 110 (MSC) and 118% (VSMC). At the max concentration of 250 µg/mL, the cell viability decreased to approximately 75 (MSC) and 97% (VSMC). An analysis of PW-CD proved to have an acceptable range of cell viability across the study. At a concentration of 10 µg/mL, the PW-CDs exhibited a value of 96 (MSC) and 99% (VSMC). Likewise, when increasing the concentration to 100 µg/mL the cell viability changed to 81 (MSC) and 96% (VSMC). At the upper limit of concentration (250 µg/mL), the cell viability of the PW-CDs dropped to 78 (MSC) and 90% (VSMC). Of the three P-CDs tested, F-CD was the most toxic, significantly reducing viability in both cells as the concentration increased. At 10 µg/mL, the initial cell viability for F-CDs were 90 (MSC) and 105% (VSMC), respectively. The cell viability was observed to drop noticeably as the concentration was increased to 100 µg/mL with values of 80 (MSC) and 94% (VSMC). At the final concentration of 250 µg/mL, the F-CDs displayed the lowest cell viability of 68 (MSC) and 80% (VSMC). Overall, the toxicity of the P-CDs was quite low and should be considered as a potential candidate to be administered as a relatively non-toxic red-emissive PS in vivo for imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Red fluorescent imaging capabilities of P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the prospect of utilizing P-CDs for imaging, the embryo zebrafish model was selected due to their physiological homology to humans. This complex vertebrae species possesses receptors, hormones and major neurotransmitters which enable researchers to develop therapeutic agents analogous to that in humans [58]. Zebrafish are advantageous to study due to their physical attributes such as small size, transparent body, and conditions to breed. In summary, the zebrafish is a feasible model to both modulate and follow non-invasive imaging techniques than that of mice [59,60]. For this experiment, embryo zebrafish were used at 32 hpf. To preface, studying embryonic development serves as a valuable model for assessing the toxicity, damage to living systems, transportation, and compatibility of nanoparticles [61]. Additionally, the unique external development of zebrafish embryos enables straightforward visual identification of abnormal embryonic mortality and poorly formed characteristics. Likewise with adults, zebrafish embryos are transparent, simplifying the real-time observation of fluorescent CDs. The embryos were soaked according to the experimental details with P-CDs (F-CD,DAEB-CD, and PW-CD) and their red fluorescence quantified after 3 hrs.

Upon excitation of the zebrafish embryo at 405 nm, there was a very faint hint of red autofluorescence observed in the control ( Fig. 5A) which is a common feature of zebrafish embryo under light excitation. As indicated in the figure, the autofluorescence was found to be in the yolk sac of the embryo. Upon analysis of the zebrafish embryos, all P-CDs were observed to be located in the yolk sac of the embryos. These findings are in agreement with what has been observed in similar reports involving CDs and zebrafish embryos [62]. Specifically, the P-CDs tend to enter the zebrafish embryos and are accumulated in the yolk sack due to the small size of the CD. Moving forward, the analysis of F-CDs revealed a much higher red fluorescence (Fig. 5B). The rationale for this observation is supported by the fact that F-CD not only appears to be the most fluorescent of all the P-CDs, but also has the smallest size range as confirmed by both AFM and TEM measurements. Following suite to strengthen the argument, DAEB-CDs having the largest particle size range, were observed to have a much more dimmed fluorescence (Fig. 5C). Interestingly, PW-CDs who are slightly smaller than DAEB-CDs, were found to have a higher degree of red fluorescence (Fig. 5D). To develop a better understanding of the fluorescence of the P-CDs in the embryos compared to the control, the mean fluorescence intensity and fold increase of CTF were determined as described in Supporting Information (Fig. 5E-F). The mean fluorescence of the control was found to have a value of 3.7 due to the existence of auto fluorescence. F-CDs, being the most fluorescent material, had an associated mean fluorescence value of 17.1. DAEB-CDs having the lowest fluorescence, had a mean fluorescence value of 9.3. Last, PW-CDs displayed a mean fluorescence value of 12.8 which was slightly higher than DAEB-CD. Considering the value of the auto fluorescence of the control, the CTF was calculated to determine the fold-increase of fluorescence intensity in the zebrafish embryo that was soaked in each P-CD. Compared to the control, F-CDs were found to have a 4.6-fold increase of fluorescence, DAEB-CDs a 2.5-fold increase, and PW-CDs a 3.5-fold increase, respectively. These values demonstrate quantitatively that these P-CDs, in proof of concept, are the ideal candidate for further studies as a red-emitting PS.

In conclusion, this study has successfully unraveled the intricate self-assembly process of diverse P-CDs achieved by pi-stacking interactions among dipyrrolomethane derivatives, coupled with the application of microwave irradiation. As a result of these controlled conditions, the formation of far-red emitting porphyrin moieties at distinct wavelengths, specifically between 650 to 715 nm, has been achieved. The initial P-CD synthesis endeavor yielded two distinct fractions: organic solvent-exclusive soluble CDs known as PO-CDs, and their water-soluble counterparts referred to as PW-CDs. Furthermore, the investigation delved into the influence of fluorine derivatives and meso-substituted ligands. Lastly, the study demonstrated the feasibility of modifying initially water-insoluble PO-CDs into water-soluble counterparts, designated as PCD-PEI, by surface modification with PEI, while retaining their distinctive red emissive attributes. To our satisfaction, the three main P-CDs (F-,DAEB, and PW-CDs) were found to be highly non-toxic in the confines of acceptable concentrations. Furthermore, these P-CDs were successful in providing red fluorescence imaging of zebrafish embryos. This revelation is important as it implies that these CDs are ideal candidates to be tested in the future in vivo applications. These findings not only expanded our understanding of P-CD synthesis but also offer promising avenues for future research and practical utilization. To our knowledge, this is the first account of porphyrin like CDs to be studied with multiple derivates characterized and used in application. We believe that these materials can be used for enhanced imaging in the far-red region as well as be studies as a potential candidate for photodynamic therapy and reactive oxygen species (ROS) production/scavenging methodologies [63,64].
